patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in the perf record command that causes it to abort when compressing multiple records using Zstd compression. The patches address three issues: an 'abort recording' error, an output buffer overrun, and a broken error fallback. The solution involves reworking the output buffer check and handling errors more robustly.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch prevents the perf record tool from storing raw data when ZSTD compression fails, which would lead to incorrect results and potential buffer overflows. Instead of copying the uncompressed input into the output buffer, it propagates the error. This fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch that added support for Zstd streaming-based compression.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Zstd compression API used by perf record, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The fix involves passing the remaining space in the output buffer to a new function process_comp_header(), which checks if the record header and padding would fit before writing them. If not, it returns -1 to stop compressing. This prevents the API from writing past the end of the output buffer, fixing an underflow bug introduced by a previous patch.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are written to the perf.data file when using Zstd compression, leading to mis-framed and decompressible data. The fix involves changing how the compressed record is framed, ensuring that each record has its own header and padding, rather than trying to fit it into a single record. This change also reduces the maximum record size by 8 bytes to prevent overflow issues.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/mm_init: handle alloc_percpu failure in free_area_init_core_hotplug

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/mm_init: handle alloc_percpu failure in free_area_init_core_hotplug

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a memory leak in the Linux kernel's memory hotplug functionality by handling failed allocations of per-cpu statistics structures. When allocating these structures, if the allocation fails, the patch propagates the -ENOMEM error up the stack and leaves the per_cpu_nodestats pointer pointing to the boot_nodestats structure, allowing a later online operation to retry the allocation. The patch modifies the free_area_init_core_hotplug function to return an integer indicating success or failure, and updates related functions to handle this new return value.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 00/10] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 00/10] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/10] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called memory_block_aligned_range() to the Linux kernel's mm/memory module. The function takes a physical address range as input and returns an aligned range, where the start address is rounded up and the end address is rounded down to memory block boundaries. This is necessary for memory hotplug operations, which require ranges to be aligned to memory blocks. The patch also updates the dax/kmem driver to use this new helper function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/10] mm/memory_hotplug: add mhp_online_type_to_str() and export string helpers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two functions to the Linux kernel: mhp_online_type_to_str() and its inverse mhp_online_type_from_str(). The new function allows drivers to convert memory online types into human-readable strings, while the existing function does the opposite. This enables drivers to render and parse memory online types through their own sysfs interfaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/10] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block` function to accept the online type through an argument, rather than calling a default value internally. This change prepares for future patches that will allow callers to specify explicit online types. The patch also updates the `add_memory_resource` function to pass the default online type via a local variable. There is no functional change in this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/10] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to access this function even when hotplug is disabled. This change enables drivers that use DAX (Direct Access) to build and function correctly in scenarios where hotplug is not enabled. The function itself simply returns `MMOP_OFFLINE`, which is a non-destructive option, ensuring compatibility with existing code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/10] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, __add_memory_driver_managed(), which allows drivers to select the preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver requires this feature to plumb its own policy through the dax kmem driver. The new function is restricted to in-tree modules using EXPORT_SYMBOL_FOR_MODULES() and does not override the system-wide onlining policy. Existing APIs are refactored to use the new helper, with no functional changes for existing users.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/10] mm/memory_hotplug: add offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() to the Linux kernel's memory_hotplug module. The function takes an array of memory ranges and processes them as one operation under a single lock, ensuring atomicity and resolving a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource. It first offlines every block in all ranges, then removes the ranges only if all are offline, reverting the whole operation if any offline fails. This provides callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step, leaving the device in a consistent state even after a failed or interrupted unplug. The existing offline_and_remove_memory() function is updated to be a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/10] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability for drivers using dax_kmem to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. It introduces a new field, 'online_type', which can be set to override the system default online policy. The system default is preserved and can still be used by setting it explicitly. This change allows for more flexibility in handling hotplug events and ensures that the ABI remains consistent even if the system default changes over time.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/10] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting initialization, cleanup, hotplug, and hot-remove logic into separate helper functions. The helpers enable future extensions to support more granular control over memory hotplug operations. The patch splits hotplug/hotunplug and init/cleanup to make resources available for hot-add, allowing re-use of the hotunplug code without destroying underlying resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/10] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug operations in DAX kmem devices. The new 'state' attribute allows for atomic transitions between online, offline, and unplugged states, addressing a race condition in the legacy two-step process. The patch also includes fixes for consistency and resource handling.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Attached a fixup patch to address a false-positive issue and added consistency to the sysfs interface. The patch also includes fixes for resource handling in case of total failure during hotplug.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 05:18:41PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> There is no atomic mechanism to offline and remove an entire
> multi-block DAX kmem device.  This is presently done in two steps:

... snip snip snip ...

Sashiko pointed out a false-positive, but adding a fixup patch
here that adds additional consistency.

On total failure - release all resources.  This makes the sysfs
interface consistent with the probe failure path.

Just attaching a fixup here, since technically it's not a bug,
could fold in separately

~Gregory
>From e341860c83fd5d5e8549d8adbf71a484c8990c5f Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Date: Tue, 30 Jun 2026 14:49:06 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] fixup! dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device
 hotplug

---
 drivers/dax/kmem.c | 5 ++++-
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/drivers/dax/kmem.c b/drivers/dax/kmem.c
index 19effe0da3dc..f597d8a99c1f 100644
--- a/drivers/dax/kmem.c
+++ b/drivers/dax/kmem.c
@@ -380,8 +380,11 @@ static ssize_t state_store(struct device *dev, struct device_attribute *attr,
 		return rc;
 
 	rc = dax_kmem_do_hotplug(dev_dax, data, online_type);
-	if (rc < 0)
+	if (rc < 0) {
+		/* Total failure, drop the reservations we took. */
+		dax_kmem_cleanup_resources(dev_dax, data);
 		return rc;
+	}
 
 	data->state = online_type;
 	return len;
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/10] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to verify the functionality of dax/kmem hotplug sysfs attributes. The test checks that transitioning a kmem-backed dax device between 'unplugged', 'online' and 'online_movable' states works as expected, including idempotence and correct memory management. It also includes a destructive test to ensure force-unbinding does not deadlock. The test discovers an existing kmem-bound dax device or skips if none are available. The patch covers specific regression testing for the online -> unplug -> online_movable -> unplug sequence.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a sysfs attribute called 'state' for atomic whole-device hotplug control in the dax/kmem driver. It allows for atomic transitions of an entire device's memory region, eliminating the need for two-step offline and remove operations that create a race condition with other userland controllers.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/vmstat: fold stranded per-cpu node stats when a node comes online

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/vmstat: fold stranded per-cpu node stats when a node comes online

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where per-node vmstat counters become stranded when a node is marked offline and then comes back online. The existing code would zero out the per-cpu counters, causing permanent skew in the statistics. This patch instead folds the stranded deltas into the global counter before the node rejoins the online set, ensuring accurate statistics. The fix was discovered during testing of node compaction on a nearly empty node, where the math for throttling broke due to the skewed stats. The patch has been tested with repeated memory hotplug/unplug cycles and has been found to resolve the issue.

Author acknowledged that folding stranded per-cpu node stats when a node comes online is complex and potentially racy, citing concerns about deadlocks, in-flight folds, and atomicity issues with this_cpu_xchg and this_cpu_add operations.

Author acknowledged that hot-unplug can leave per-cpu vmstat deltas unfolded, and explained that visiting all possible CPUs is necessary due to the possibility of CPUs going offline while the node was offline.

sentiment_signals: acknowledged complexity of folding stranded per-cpu node stats
cited potential for deadlocks and in-flight fold risks
review_comments.raw_body:
I tried this first.  I was unable to convince myself there was a 
safe way to accomplish this.

1) sashiko pointed out we can't schedule_on_each_cpu while holding
   the hotplug lock because we'll re-take cpus_read_lock and cause
   a deadlock condition with cpu-hotplug.

2) I'm not sure we can do it after the hotplug lock as been dropped,
   at least not safely.  At the very least another hot-plug re-adding
   the node could start.  That just seemed like a bad path.

3) foreign cpu access to the per-cpu values are not atomic with
   respect to in-flight folds on the target cpu.  this_cpu_xchg
   and this_cpu_add are (i believe) only atomic wrt the cpu itself
   (can't be interrupted mid-exchange).

doing it before node_offline() has problems (in-flight folds),
doing it after node_offline() still *technically* carries the same
in-flight fold risk - just narrower (fold has to have started already).

I couldn't convince myself there wasn't still a race, so here we are.

---

Unfortunately - cpus may have gone offline while the node was offline,
so we legitimately have to visit every *possible* cpu :[

---

I am aware :[.  I suppose we could vectorize the collection here on some
archs, but I try to avoid being clever where I can.

---

Will take a look, thanks!

~Gregory

From: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Subject: mm/vmstat: fold stranded per-cpu node stats when a node comes online
Date: Sat, 27 Jun 2026 16:22:43 -0400

A per-node vmstat counter is pgdat->vm_stat[] plus per-cpu deltas.  A
balanced counter can sit split as global=+N / per-cpu=-N.

The folds reconciling the split only walk online nodes, so when
try_offline_node() marks a node offline the per-cpu deltas are stranded.

A subsequent online resets the per-cpu area but not pgdat->vm_stat[],
orphaning the +N permanently.  All NR_VM_NODE_STAT_ITEMS are affected.

The existing code zeroes the per-cpu counters and causes a permanent skew.
Fold the stranded deltas instead, before the node rejoins the online set.
The node is not online yet and the hotplug lock is held, so the remote
access to per-cpu values is safe.

Discovered when node compaction hung for a nearly empty node, as the math
to determine throttling broke.  Reproduced by repeated memory
hotplug/unplug cycles on a node under pressure: NR_ISOLATED_ANON ratchets
up and never returns to zero.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/20260627202243.758289-1-gourry@gourry.net
Fixes: 75ef71840539 ("mm, vmstat: add infrastructure for per-node vmstats")
Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Cc: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
Cc: Mel Gorman <mgorman@techsingularity.net>
Cc: Mike Rapoport <rppt@kernel.org>
Cc: Vlastimil Babka <vbabka@kernel.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
---

 mm/mm_init.c |   15 +++++++++++----
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

--- a/mm/mm_init.c~mm-vmstat-fold-stranded-per-cpu-node-stats-when-a-node-comes-online
+++ a/mm/mm_init.c
@@ -1540,7 +1540,7 @@ void __ref free_area_init_core_hotplug(s
 {
 	int nid = pgdat->node_id;
 	enum zone_type z;
-	int cpu;
+	int cpu, i;
 
 	pgdat_init_internals(pgdat);
 
@@ -1558,10 +1558,17 @@ void __ref free_area_init_core_hotplug(s
 	pgdat->node_start_pfn = 0;
 	pgdat->node_present_pages = 0;
 
-	for_each_online_cpu(cpu) {
-		struct per_cpu_nodestat *p;
+	/*
+	 * Hot-unplug can leave per-cpu vmstat deltas unfolded (folders skip
+	 * offline nodes) - reconcile this at online. Foreign access to counters
+	 * is safe: the node is not online yet and we hold the hotplug lock.
+	 */
+	for_each_possible_cpu(cpu) {
+		struct per_cpu_nodestat *p = per_cpu_ptr(pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, cpu);
 
-		p = per_cpu_ptr(pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, cpu);
+		for (i = 0; i < NR_VM_NODE_STAT_ITEMS; i++)
+			if (p->vm_node_stat_diff[i])
+				node_page_state_add(p->vm_node_stat_diff[i], pgdat, i);
 		memset(p, 0, sizeof(*p));
 	}
 
_

Author acknowledged that the hotplug lock is held when a node comes online, making it safe to access per-cpu counters and fold stranded deltas.

review_comments.author: Hannes Reinecke
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issue of conflicting udev rules and uevent generation for both interfaces. Suggested disabling uevents for the old interface or making it configurable.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 7/1/26 12:14 AM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 05:18:41PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
>> There is no atomic mechanism to offline and remove an entire
>> multi-block DAX kmem device.  This is presently done in two steps:
> 
> ... snip snip snip ...
> 
> Sashiko pointed out a false-positive, but adding a fixup patch
> here that adds additional consistency.
> 
> On total failure - release all resources.  This makes the sysfs
> interface consistent with the probe failure path.
> 

Speaking of which ...
With this patch we now have _two_ interfaces to do the same thing.
And both will be generating uevents.
Which is far from ideal (one could easily envision _conflicting_
udev rules, one set doing an 'online' on the old interface,
and another set doing an 'offline' on the new interface...)
Is there a way to not sending uevents for the old interface
or to make it configurable?
The old interface had the nasty side effect of generating
_tons_ of uevents during booting, and on larger machines we
even had seen udev acting as a fork-bomb during booting,
taking down the entire machine (we had to restrict udev
to 512 threads max to avoid that from happening).
So if we could disable uevents for the old interface
things would be _so_ much easier ...

Cheers,

Hannes

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/4] sunrpc: protect the svc_pool_map pool_to[] array with RCU

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/4] sunrpc: protect the svc_pool_map pool_to[] array with RCU

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch protects the svc_pool_map pool_to[] array from concurrent access by readers and writers without proper synchronization. It uses RCU (Read-Copy Update) to ensure that readers can safely access the array even when it is being updated or torn down. The patch also updates the code to use rcu_assign_pointer() and kfree_rcu_mightsleep() to properly manage the pool_to[] array's lifetime. This fix addresses a problem where svc_pool_map_get_node() could dereference a partially built or already freed pool_to[] array, leading to potential crashes.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown

svc_create_pooled() is used, svc_pool_map_get() is called early so a

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `svc_pool_for_cpu` function to route RPC transports to a populated pool instead of one that may be empty due to uneven thread distribution across pools. This ensures that incoming connections are always serviced, even if some pools have no threads assigned to them. The change trades off NUMA locality for guaranteed serviceability of transports.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, which is considered the right choice everywhere. It removes other modes such as SVC_POOL_AUTO/GLOBAL/PERCPU/PERNODE and simplifies pool map functions. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts changes to one pool per NUMA node by default. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility, but writing any previously-accepted value succeeds silently and reading always returns "pernode". The patch also updates the documentation to mark the pool_mode parameter deprecated and describe the new behavior.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the removal of prefix matching in __param_set_pool_mode() causes previously accepted values like 'globalx' to be rejected with -EINVAL, and requested a fix to ensure backwards compatibility

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the kdoc modification in Documentation/admin-guide/kernel-parameters.txt is inconsistent, as it still refers to 'cpus to pools' despite percpu mode being removed and the patch correcting struct field comments to reflect a node-to-pool mapping.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that an unpooled svc_new_thread() running concurrently with an nfsd start or stop may observe m->count != 0 while pool_to is still NULL or already freed, and dereference it in svc_pool_map_put(), requesting the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap().

The reviewer noted that the patch introduces a potential dereference of pool_to[] when m->count is non-zero, and svc_new_thread() calls svc_pool_map_get_node(pool->sp_id) without holding any necessary locks or references.

Reviewer noted that pool_to[pidx] is dereferenced before the m->count == 0 check, making the WARN/return in svc_pool_map_get() dead code due to the sv_nrpools > 1 gate at the call site.
review_comments.raw_body:
sysfs_match_string() compares the whole string, whereas the old
__param_set_pool_mode() matched prefixes (strncmp(val, "global", 6)
and friends). An input like "globalx" that the old code accepted is
now rejected with -EINVAL.

The commit message says:

  Writing any previously-accepted value succeeds silently

The four documented names still succeed, including with a trailing
newline since sysfs_streq() tolerates one, so only prefix-extended
strings change.

---

Regarding the kdoc modification above: with percpu removed the
map is node-to-pool; "cpus to pools" now contradicts the struct
field comments this same patch corrected ("maps pool id to
node" / "maps node to pool id").

---

svc_pool_map_put() frees pool_to[] and sets it back to NULL when the
last pooled reference drops.

Can an unpooled svc_new_thread() running concurrently with an nfsd
start or stop observe m->count != 0 while pool_to is still NULL (the
first get) or already freed (the last put), and dereference it here?
The reads of m->count and m->pool_to are not under the mutex.

The old default global mode returned numa_mem_id() in this case and
never touched pool_to[]; with pernode as the default this path runs
for every server. Would it help to consult pool_to[] only when the
service holds a reference, the way svc_pool_map_set_cpumask() is
already gated in svc_new_thread()?

        if (serv->sv_nrpools > 1)
                svc_pool_map_set_cpumask(task, pool->sp_id);

An unpooled service (sv_nrpools == 1) could then take numa_mem_id()
without reading the shared map.

---

This path now dereferences pool_to[] whenever m->count is non-zero.

svc_new_thread() calls it for every service, pooled or not, holding
neither svc_pool_map_mutex nor a map reference:

        node = svc_pool_map_get_node(pool->sp_id);

Unpooled services reach this too: lockd and the NFS callback are both
created with svc_create(), which passes npools = 1 and never calls
svc_pool_map_get(), so their m->count is whatever a pooled nfsd has
left set.

svc_pool_map_get() bumps the count before pool_to[] is allocated, and
the whole sequence is serialized only against other map writers:

        mutex_lock(&svc_pool_map_mutex);
        if (m->count++) {
                ...
        }
        npools = svc_pool_map_init_pernode(m);   /* allocates pool_to */

---

pool_to[pidx] is dereferenced before the m->count == 0 check, so if
count were ever 0 the NULL deref has already happened and the
WARN/return can never fire. The sv_nrpools > 1 gate at the call site
keeps this safe, so the guard is now dead code.

For the series as a whole, let's consider moving 5/4 to the front.
It looks like a fix that might want to be backported.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per CPU-bearing node when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that each CPU-bearing node in a NUMA system has at least one thread when distributing RPC threads automatically. When the requested number of threads is less than the number of pools, some nodes might have empty pools due to even distribution. This patch floors each pool's thread count at one if it belongs to a CPU-bearing node, preventing transports from being steered to empty pools. The total number of threads may exceed the requested count as a result. This change only affects automatic thread distribution and does not impact explicit per-pool thread counts set by callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] sunrpc: eliminate a modulus operation from the enqueueing codepath
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the sunrpc enqueueing code by replacing a costly modulus operation with an explicit check for array bounds. The original code used a modulus to determine which pool to enqueue on, but this has been replaced with a simple branch that checks if the index is within the valid range. If it's not, the code falls back to pool 0. This change reduces the number of cycles required and improves performance.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series hardcodes the pool_mode to pernode, removing other modes. It addresses a shortcoming in the existing code where RPCs can be scheduled to pools with no threads. The patches also include performance optimizations and improvements to thread auto-placement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't let shrinker touch extent_maps that are being logged

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't let shrinker touch extent_maps that are being logged

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Btrfs extent map shrinker that can cause corruption and RCU stalls by freeing an extent map that is still being logged. The fix involves adding a check to skip extent maps that are being logged, similar to how pinned maps are skipped. This prevents a use-after-free situation where a later fsync operation tries to access the freed extent map. The patch addresses a problem that has been observed in production environments on both ARM64 and x86_64 architectures.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Filipe Manana questioned the patch's logic, pointing out that fsync takes the inode's i_mmap_lock in write/exclusive mode and btrfs_scan_inode also takes this lock in shared/read mode, making it unlikely for the shrinker to race with fsync. He also corrected a minor syntax error.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't see how that can happen, because fsync always takes the
inode's i_mmap_lock (in write/exclusive mode), and btrfs_scan_inode()
takes the same lock too (in shared/read mode).
EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING is only set and cleared during fsync, so how can
the shrinker race with fsync?

Also, there should be no () after modified_extents (it's not a function name).

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the i_mmap_lock interlock is incomplete and can't guarantee that LOGGING won't be set on extent maps after taking the lock, which contradicts the original invariant. The author provided analysis showing that tree-log.c never takes i_mmap_lock, but btrfs_sync_file does only for the inode being fsync'd, leaving other inodes vulnerable to having their ems' LOGGING flag set while the shrinker is running.
review_comments.raw_body:
...and yet, the vmcore shows that the shrinker hit an extent_map that
had LOGGING set while holding the i_mmap_lock.

I had hoped to add an assertion here that LOGGING couldn't be set on
the em's after taking the i_mmap_lock, but that's not viable:

The problem is that the i_mmap_lock coverage isn't complete. I had
Claude assist me with some analysis so take it with a hefty grain of
salt but the problem is that the em tree can contain em's from other
inodes where the i_mmap_lock is not held:

-----------------------8<---------------------

...that inode is in progress, therefore no em in its tree should have LOGGING set. Your invariant is the right one.

 But it's violated, because the i_mmap_lock interlock is incomplete. Two facts:                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  1. tree-log.c never takes i_mmap_lock — grep returns 0 occurrences. Only btrfs_sync_file takes it (file.c:1608/1610), and only                                                                                         
  on the one inode being fsync'd.                                                                                                                                                                                        
  2. A single fsync logs many other inodes, and each can set LOGGING on its own ems via btrfs_log_changed_extents().                                                                                                     
  btrfs_log_inode() is called recursively on conflicting inodes, parent directories, and referenced inodes:                                                                                                              
  tree-log.c:5982  btrfs_log_inode(di_inode, log_mode)        # dir dentries                                                                                                                                             
  tree-log.c:6339  btrfs_log_inode(inode, LOG_INODE_ALL)      # conflicting inodes                                                                                                                                       
  tree-log.c:6900  btrfs_log_inode(di_inode, log_mode)        # new dir dentries                                                                                                                                         
  tree-log.c:7389  btrfs_log_inode(dir_inode, LOG_INODE_ALL)  # log_all_parents                                                                                                                                          
  tree-log.c:7488  btrfs_log_inode(inode, ...)                # referenced inodes                                                                                                                                        

  2. For all of these, btrfs_sync_file holds i_mmap_lock on the original fsync'd inode — not on these side inodes. So a side                                                                                             
  inode X gets LOGGING set on its ems while X's own i_mmap_lock is free.                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  So the shrinker can down_read_trylock(X->i_mmap_lock) successfully (nobody holds it) and walk X's tree while X's ems are                                                                                               
  LOGGING-flagged → it removes+frees one → corruption. The interlock only protects the directly-fsync'd inode, not the inodes                                                                                            
  logged as its dependencies.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the em tree should only contain extent maps for its owning inode, and suggested investigating why the buggy behavior occurs elsewhere in the code
review_comments.raw_body:
And that is the problem: an em tree, which belongs to an inode, should
only have extent maps for that inode...
An extent map can only belong to an inode.

So that's what needs investigation. The bug is elsewhere.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that while a single fsync can log other inodes without taking their i_mmap_lock, the extents are not logged due to logging being done in LOG_INODE_EXISTS mode, except for symlink inodes which are always logged in full mode and have BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set.

Reviewer Filipe Manana questioned the patch's assumption that extent maps being logged would be skipped by the shrinker, pointing out that there is no actual code path where an inode being fsynced in LOG_INODE_ALL mode would log another inode. He noted an exception for symlinks, which always have BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set.
review_comments.raw_body:
A single fsync can log other inodes yes, and without taking their
i_mmap_lock, but the inodes are logged in LOG_INODE_EXISTS mode, so
extents are not logged.
The only exception is for symlink inodes, but those are logged in full
mode, only have one extent (inlined), and always have
BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC set, so we never end up in
btrfs_log_changed_extents() for symlinks.

---

Nop. Unless you can show an actual code path where while fsyncing one
inode we log another inode in LOG_INODE_ALL mode
Again, the exception is symlinks, but for those, the
BTRFS_INODE_NEEDS_FULL_SYNC flag is always set, so we never end up in
btrfs_log_changed_extents().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the shrinker needs to handle logging extent maps, agreed to add warnings and potentially BUG() calls to prevent UAF situations.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the explanation. It sounds like we should wire some warnings
into the shrinker around these invariants. Like, if LOGGING is set and
the inode's not a symlink, do a WARN_ON_ONCE()? I'll plan to look into
that as well.

FWIW, internally at Meta, we're turning the WARN_ON() calls in
btrfs_free_extent_map() into BUG() calls across a swath of machines in
the hopes we can get a vmcore earlier. We'll keep you posted.

Thanks for the help so far!

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that logging of extent maps requires holding both i_mmap_lock and vfs lock on the inode, and that the shrinker should skip extent maps with the LOGGING flag to prevent corruption and data loss due to potential races with writes or other operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
To make it as simple as possible:

An fsync can only log extent maps for the inode being fsynced; other
inodes can be logged, but not their extent maps.
In order to log extent maps, we need to have the i_mmap_lock (in
write/exclusive mode) and the vfs lock on the inode too.

Symlinks have an inline extent but no extent map.
Directories never have extents.

So if the shrinker gets an extent map with the LOGGING flag, one of
two things is happening:

1) An fsync of one inode is logging extent maps of another. This bug
can cause corruption and data loss, because we must hold those two
locks to prevent races with writes (buffered, direct IO, mmap) and
other operations (truncate, hole punching, etc.), as previously
stated.

2) Something is corrupting an extent map, either inside or outside
(most likely) the fsync code.

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/6] fuse: decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the creation of fuse_ring from entry registration in FUSE connections. The ring is now created during initialization after a server has negotiated and set FUSE_OVER_IO_URING, rather than being lazily created on the first FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER command. This change eliminates concurrent creation races and simplifies the code by removing open-coded handling in fuse_uring_create().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/6] fuse: add FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE command to the Linux kernel's fuse module, allowing servers to create queues explicitly and decoupling queue setup from entry registration. This change is necessary for the introduction of buffer pools, which require existing queues to be created first. The patch modifies the fuse_ring_queue creation logic to handle explicit queue creation and adds a new function fuse_uring_add_queue to handle this command.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous chunk of memory to the kernel for servicing requests. This decouples the buffer allocation from individual entries and enables the kernel to optimize memory usage by reusing buffers across multiple requests. The patch introduces a new command, FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL, for registering a buffer pool per queue, and modifies the io-uring implementation to use a bitmap to track free buffers and report their byte offset to the server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/6] fuse: support registered buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use a shared buffer pool that is also registered through io-uring. This eliminates the overhead of pinning and unpining user pages per I/O request, improving performance on write-intensive workloads. To use this feature, servers must set the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFPOOL flag when adding a bufpool and include the index of the registered buffer pool in every submitted sqe. The patch includes benchmark results showing improved throughput for direct writes using registered buffers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/6] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. This is achieved by registering relevant pages into a sparse slot in the server's io_uring registered buffer table and using fixed buffer operations. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN capability and bufpools, and introduces a new flag FOPEN_IO_URING_ZERO_COPY for servers to specify which open files should use zero-copy. The request flow is modified to skip data copying when possible, reducing memory overhead and improving performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/6] docs: fuse: document io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy uapi
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for the Fuse over io-uring usage of buffer pools and zero-copy functionality. It introduces new commands in the uapi (user-space API) to manage buffer pools and enable zero-copy, which requires administrative privileges and a registered buffer pool. The documentation provides details on how to use these features, including setting up buffer pools and enabling zero-copy for optimal performance.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy to the FUSE file system, allowing servers to pass in a contiguous chunk of memory that the kernel can use for servicing requests. This decouples the request entries from their payload buffers, reducing memory usage requirements and enabling optimizations for assigning payload memory. The series also includes zero-copy support, which allows direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using buffer pools.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where mapped shared memory (shmem) was not considered for NUMA balancing on tiered systems. The issue arose from a previous commit that intended to limit NUMA scanning to migrate-on-fault VMAs, but incorrectly treated the absence of a shared policy as an explicit opt-out. The patch changes the vma_policy_mof function to use __get_vma_policy directly, allowing default policy VMAs to participate in balancing while still excluding explicitly pinned memory. This fix should improve performance on tiered systems with cachelib-based workloads.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gregory Price expressed strong disagreement (CONTENTIOUS) with the patch, stating that demotions don't care about mempolicy and that opting shmem out of NUMA balancing on a tiered system is problematic. He also emphasized that this issue highlights the need for a complete overhaul of the demotion mechanism to prevent undefined behavior in memory placement.

Gave Reviewed-by
review_comments.raw_body:
Eugh.

Demotions don't care about mempolicy, so opting shmem out of NUMA
balancing and mbind'ing on a tiered system is just full sadness.

This is all just more evidence that demotion needs to be completely
redone, it's creating a mess of undefined behavior for memory placement.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)

review_comments.raw_body:
Right, mbinding in tiered mode is a whole other ball of wax. I'm just
trying to make the default case work ;-)

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch's change to use __get_vma_policy() directly creates a fallback to get_task_policy() when vma->vm_ops->get_policy returns NULL, which was not the case before and may cause confusion.
review_comments.raw_body:
Okay, we used the fallback of vma->vm_policy before (if vma->vm_ops->get_policy
was not available), which is what __get_vma_policy() does at well.

But if vma->vm_ops->get_policy now returns NULL, we fallback to get_task_policy().


Makes sense to me although this is a source of confusion for me.

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges the patch is addressing an old issue, but one that still affects real workloads on tiered systems, and agrees it's a straightforward bug that can be easily backported.
review_comments.raw_body:
No objection from me. I was hesitant because it's old, and while these
are real workloads that see it they are hardware/kernel validation
runs. OTOH it's a straight-forward bug and should backport easily.

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Huang noted that the patch does not handle the case where a vma's policy is NULL, and suggested adding a check for this condition to avoid potential crashes.

reviewer noted that the current implementation of mempolicy has issues during demotion and requested suggestions for improvement

[snip]

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Gregory Price suggested respecting per-vma mempolicies instead of per-task policies, which would make the interaction less painful and allow mbind() to behave as expected. He also noted that task-wide mempolicy is problematic on tiered systems and proposed documenting this limitation.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think it's feasible we could respect per-vma mempolicies, but not
per-task.  That would at least make this particular interaction less
painful and mbind() would do what you'd expect.  It is a bit racy,
but with MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL the user can get what they actually want.

I think task-wide mempolicy is problematic and generally a bad idea
on tiered systems, maybe it's ok if we simply document task policies
are not respected on tiered systems?

~Gregory

On 30/06/2026 17:07, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 04:23:32AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
>> vmpressure() has two outputs gated by the @tree argument:
>>
>>   @tree=false drives in-kernel socket pressure (mem_cgroup_set_
>>               socket_pressure), consumed by TCP/SCTP. This only
>>               applies on cgroup v2; on v1 socket memory is charged
>>               separately via tcpmem and the consumer reads
>>               memcg->tcpmem_pressure instead.
>>
>>   @tree=true  drives userspace eventfd notifications via the v1

>>               v2 has no equivalent: userspace gets reclaim signals
>>               through memory.pressure (PSI), which does not touch
>>               vmpressure.

>> The existing early return covered v1 + @tree=false. The symmetric
>> v2 + @tree=true case was falling through and doing the full lock /
>> accumulate / schedule_work / parent-walk dance for an events list
>> that can never be populated. bpftrace on a 176-core production host
>> (cgroup v2, CONFIG_MEMCG_V1=n, 285 memcgs, sustained reclaim) showed
>> ~16,200 @tree=true vmpressure() calls per minute. Add an early return
>> that skips cgroup v2 + tree = true which avoids us doing all this work.
>> On a v2-only host this also eliminates a lock contention path that can
>> serialise reclaimers on a single global sr_lock.
>>
>> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

>> +++ b/mm/vmpressure.c
>> @@ -246,11 +246,13 @@ void vmpressure(gfp_t gfp, int order, struct mem_cgroup *memcg, bool tree,
>>  		return;
>>  
>>  	/*
>> -	 * The in-kernel users only care about the reclaim efficiency
>> -	 * for this @memcg rather than the whole subtree, and there
>> -	 * isn't and won't be any in-kernel user in a legacy cgroup.
>> +	 * Only two combinations have a consumer:
>> +	 *   cgroup v2 + tree=false -> in-kernel socket pressure
>> +	 *   cgroup v1 + tree=true  -> userspace eventfds (memory.pressure_level)
>> +	 * Skip the other two: nothing consumes the result.
>>  	 */
>> -	if (!cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys) && !tree)
>> +	if ((!cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys) && !tree) ||
>> +	    (cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys) && tree))
>>  		return;

> Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Yeah sorry about this! I just amended my last patch to move code
from vmpressure-v1.c to memcontrol-v1.c and just sent it, without
other changes. Forgot Shakeels ack on v2 as well :(

Andrew would you mind applying the below fixlet? I can also respin
if its easier. Thanks!!

>From 969c19da782bbcd77ae4b9e94d3a9e1d78c198d7 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Date: Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:25:05 -0700
Subject: [fixlet] mm/vmpressure: skip tree=true accounting on cgroup v2

Simplify the guard. Both cgroup_subsys_on_dfl() and tree are bool, so
the two combinations that have no consumer (v1 + tree=false, v2 +
tree=true) are exactly the cases where dfl == tree.

Suggested-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/vmpressure.c | 3 +--
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/mm/vmpressure.c b/mm/vmpressure.c
index 14470141bbe6..9629240d77ad 100644
--- a/mm/vmpressure.c
+++ b/mm/vmpressure.c
@@ -120,8 +120,7 @@ void vmpressure(gfp_t gfp, int order, struct mem_cgroup *memcg, bool tree,
 	 *   cgroup v1 + tree=true  -> userspace eventfds (memory.pressure_level)
 	 * Skip the other two: nothing consumes the result.
 	 */
-	if ((!cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys) && !tree) ||
-	    (cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys) && tree))
+	if (cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys) == tree)
 		return;
 
 	vmpr = memcg_to_vmpressure(memcg);
--

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort
[PATCH v5 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPUs that are wedged and ignoring interrupts. When a CPU is wedged, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI, there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event on the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF. This allows the CPU to be shut down even if it's ignoring interrupts. The patch also folds stop bookkeeping into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(), replacing near-duplicate local functions.

[PATCH v5 3/4] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux kernel that allows delivering an NMI-like event to an interrupt-masked CPU on arm64 architecture using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) software-signalled event mechanism. The goal is to provide a more efficient way to deliver events to CPUs that are currently masked, which can help in scenarios such as system crashes or hangs. The patch introduces a new driver and modifies existing code to support this feature. It also includes documentation updates and adds a new configuration option for the SDEI NMI feature.

The function takes no locks and is safe to call from NMI or crash context.

[PATCH v5 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver interrupts across CPUs on arm64 systems, specifically targeting scenarios where the CPU has its interrupts locally masked. The current pseudo-NMI and FEAT_NMI mechanisms have limitations, such as introducing a significant performance overhead or requiring architectural support that is not yet widely deployed. This series introduces SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) as a new delivery backend, which allows firmware to deliver an interrupt into a CPU regardless of its DAIF state, thus enabling the backtrace and crash-stop paths to reach a masked CPU without incurring significant performance costs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't propagate EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING to split extent maps

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't propagate EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING to split extent maps

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the btrfs extent map handling by not propagating EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING to split extent maps, which was causing a use-after-free warning when an extent map is freed while still on the inode's modified_extents list. The fix involves clearing EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING from the local 'flags' copy used for splits and only clearing EXTENT_FLAG_PINNED from em->flags.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be correct. The bug was properly identified and fixed by clearing EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING from the local 'flags' copy used for splits.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 2026-06-30 at 12:58 -0700, Leo Martins wrote:
> When btrfs_drop_extent_map_range() splits an extent map, the new split
> maps inherit the original map's flags through a local 'flags' variable.
> Commit f86f7a75e2fb ("btrfs: use the flags of an extent map to identify
> the compression type") changed the EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING clearing to
> operate on em->flags instead of that local 'flags' copy, so a split of
> an extent map that is currently being logged wrongly inherits
> EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING.
> 
> The flag is then never cleared on the split, and when it is freed while
> still on the inode's modified_extents list (for example by the extent
> map shrinker) it trips the WARN_ON(!list_empty(&em->list)) in
> btrfs_free_extent_map() and leads to a use-after-free.
> 
> Clear EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING from the local 'flags' copy used for the
> splits and only clear EXTENT_FLAG_PINNED from em->flags, restoring the
> behaviour prior to f86f7a75e2fb.
> 
> Fixes: f86f7a75e2fb ("btrfs: use the flags of an extent map to identify the compression type")
> Cc: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> Cc: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> Signed-off-by: Leo Martins <loemra.dev@gmail.com>
> ---
>  fs/btrfs/extent_map.c | 4 ++--
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> index fce9c5cc0122..6ad7b39ae358 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> @@ -866,13 +866,13 @@ void btrfs_drop_extent_map_range(struct btrfs_inode *inode, u64 start, u64 end,
>  			goto next;
>  		}
>  
> -		flags = em->flags;
>  		/*
>  		 * In case we split the extent map, we want to preserve the
>  		 * EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING flag on our extent map, but we don't want
>  		 * it on the new extent maps.
>  		 */
> -		em->flags &= ~(EXTENT_FLAG_PINNED | EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING);
> +		flags = em->flags & ~EXTENT_FLAG_LOGGING;
> +		em->flags &= ~EXTENT_FLAG_PINNED;
>  		modified = !list_empty(&em->list);
>  
>  		/*

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: replace writeback inhibition xarray with a fixed inline buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: replace writeback inhibition xarray with a fixed inline buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the xarray used to track inhibited extent buffers in Btrfs transactions with a fixed inline buffer, managed by a CLOCK eviction policy. This change addresses two issues: a write_iops regression caused by frequent xarray stores during COW operations and an unbounded cleanup walk due to long iteration over inhibited buffers. The new approach uses a small fixed-size array to track the working set of buffers revisited across search_again descents, with a CLOCK eviction policy that ensures frequently accessed buffers are retained while less frequently accessed ones are reclaimed first.

review_comments.author: Sun YangKai
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that the design is clean but suggested non-blocking improvements

Reviewer Sun YangKai suggested using an overflowing add operation with masking to replace the modulo operator when indexing into the fixed inline buffer, and questioned the usefulness of storing additional information in the higher bits.

The reviewer suggested extracting the inhibit preparation logic into a helper function, likely to improve code organization and readability.

Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that using u8 for inhibited_ebs fields is a straightforward choice, even if the limit is later extended to 16 slots, and only the bitmap field would need to be widened to u16.
sentiment_signals: no clear disagreement or request for change
review_comments.raw_body:
Reviewed-by: Sun YangKai <sunk67188@gmail.com>

Thanks for your great work. The overall direction looks nice and the 
design is clean. I've got some non\u2011blocking suggestions, though.

---

I think we can using overflowing add with ` & (SLOTS - 1)` as the index 
as long as SLOTS is power of 2, instead of `% SLOTS`. And the higher 
bits can parsed as number of "rounds" if we keep them, and I'm not sure 
if the extra info is useful for any purpose.

---

And personally I'd love to wrap this part of logic into a helper 
function like

static inline u8 btrfs_inhibit_prepare_slot(struct btrfs_trans_handle 
*trans) {...}

Or something with a better name.

---

Given the 8-slot limit for inhibited_ebs (4 bits (8) for capacity, 8 
bits for bitmap), using u8 for these two fields is a clear and 
straightforward choice IMO. Even if we later extend the limit to 16, 
only the bitmap field (inhibited_ebs_referenced) would need to be 
widened to u16.

Thanks,
Sun YangKai

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David Sterba suggested checking the compiler's optimization of immediate constants, specifically looking for strength reduction from division to bitwise operations.

Reviewer David Sterba noted that the fixed inline buffer size could be reduced from u32 to u8, potentially saving space if transaction handle sizes increase beyond what fits on common slabs.
review_comments.raw_body:
For immediate constants the compiler does various transformations that
utilize the power of two abilities to reduce the operations (I think
it's called 'strength reduce', e.g. mod -> bitwise and). You can check
the assembly if there's a division or and.

---

The u32 is a bit more convenient on the instruction level, we can
consider u8 in case the transaction handle size grows more than what
fits to common slabs.

The xarray version has 184 bytes and quick estimate of this patch is
about 240 so we're in the 256 bucket anyway.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David Sterba noted that the patch's performance improvement is significant (22% drop), but suggested pushing it to a later release cycle (7.2) and requested additional testing before inclusion in the kernel.

Reviewer David Sterba requested that the patch description be reformatted for better readability, specifically suggesting a table or splitting the paragraph by case, and also pointing out that the numbers in the text are hard to see.

Reviewer David Sterba suggested adding a comment to acknowledge the issue and its impact, but did not provide specific technical details about the fix.

Reviewer David Sterba pointed out that using an 'int' instead of a larger type for simple indexes does not affect bit shifting operations, suggesting this could simplify the code.

Reviewer David Sterba suggested using static_assert to enforce constraints on the size of the fixed inline buffer, specifically providing a power-of-two check using the expression 'n != 0 && (n & (n - 1)) == 0'.

Reviewer David Sterba pointed out that due to alignment constraints, a u32 can be used instead of a narrower type, and noted that most of the tracking is still done in the inhibited_ebs array.
review_comments.raw_body:
22% drop is quite significant, it's now in 7.1. It could be possible to
push that to 7.2 as it's fixing only the tracking mechanism. We'll need
to get it tested for some time, so estimated rc would be like rc3 or rc4
at most.

---

Can you please transform the paragraph to a table or split it by case?
The numbers are burried in text, hard to see what's the actual benefit.

---

Would be good to comment under the report that you're aware of the
issue, not necessarily say how you're fixing it or when.

---

For simple indexes we use 'int', it does not make any differences for
the bit shifts when it's the number.

---

Please use static_assert for the constraints, for power of two there's
the "n != 0 && (n & (n - 1)) == 0" trick that also works for the
assertion.

---

Due to alignment constraints you can use u32 too, thre will be a hole
after it anyway.

As mentioned in my other reply, the structure size is under 256 so we
don't have to save space with narrow types, majority of the tracking is
still in the inhibited_ebs array.
